balance_sheet.title: **Balance Sheet Overview – As of May 31, 2026**
balance_sheet.content:
The primary driver of change on Bopti Federal Credit Union’s balance sheet was a sharp contraction in loans and leases, which declined by $4.7 million (–$19,790,334 to $15,800,000 approximately) or –11.1% year-over-year — the largest absolute dollar movement on the asset side. This reduction pulled total assets down slightly from prior period levels, though they still grew 7.8% overall to reach $63,654,928.

Investment securities rose by $5.0 million (+13.2%) and now represent the largest single asset category at $39,273,170 (61.7%), expanding the non-lending portion of earning assets as loans receded. Cash and deposits surged 247.6% to $3,603,141 (+$5.8 million), significantly improving near-term liquidity positioning despite their small share of total assets.

Loans and leases now constitute just over one-third (31.1%) of the asset mix after this period’s decline — a structural shift away from traditional lending toward securities holdings. Other investments remained flat at $109,776; other assets contracted 9.1% to $878,507.

Total liabilities decreased by $2.7 million (–$47,948,105) or –5.5%, primarily driven by a drop in shares/deposits of $3.1 million (–6.3%). Accounts payable and accrued interest on borrowings increased 72.0% to $820,726 but remain minor relative to total liabilities.

Equity stood at $15,706,823 (+$264k or +1.7%), fully attributable to undivided earnings of the same amount — indicating no appropriations were taken this period and that retained capital grew organically through operations.

earning.title: Earnings: As of May 31, 2026
earning.content:
Net Income for the credit union was $265,377 in Q2'2026.

The primary earnings driver this period was Net Interest Income. With Interest Income at $1,178,992 and Interest Expense at $393,101, the resulting spread of approximately $785,891 served as the dominant contributor to profitability for the quarter.

Non-Interest Income totaled $61,283. This category was comprised primarily of Fee Income ($44,516) and Other Income ($28,579). While these components provided a modest supplement to core spread income, they represented less than 10% of total revenue for the period.

Non-Interest Expense totaled $560,366. The expense base was distributed across several categories rather than concentrated in one area; however, Employee Compensation ($44,516) and Office Occupancy Expenses ($87,671) were significant line items alongside Operating Fees ($297). Overall, the credit union's operating costs remained aligned with its revenue scale for this quarter.

Overall Position: The combination of a strong interest spread and controlled non-interest expenses produced net income of $265,377. Sustaining profitability will depend on maintaining current levels of Net Interest Income while managing Non-Interest Expense growth.

key_financial.title: Commentary on Key Financial Performance – May 31, 2026
key_financial.content:
Capital & Solvency
The credit union maintained its net worth ratio at 24.67%, reflecting a year-over-year increase of 253 basis points. The solvency ratio stood at 1.33%. This elevated capital level provides the institution with substantial capacity to absorb potential losses and supports continued lending operations.

Asset Quality
The delinquency ratio increased from 1.68% in Q1'2026 to 1.73%, while the net charge-off ratio decreased from 0.54% to 0.50%. The improvement in charge-offs indicates better collection performance or loan mix adjustments, even as a slight uptick in delinquency is observed. Continued monitoring of asset quality will be important given the concurrent rise in delinquent loans.

Profitability
Return on average assets (ROAA) was 0.95%, up from 0.91% in Q1'2026, while net interest margin improved to 2.85%. Net operating expenses as a percentage of average assets were 1.91%, down from 2.07% previously. The profitability picture is positive this period, with both margins and returns expanding alongside reduced expense ratios.

Funding & Growth
The loans-to-shares ratio was 42.25%. Loan balance growth declined by 27.52%, while shares/deposits grew at a slower pace of -11.03% year-over-year, though the core funding ratio remained stable at 57.70%. The credit union is in a defensive liquidity position this period, with both loan and share balances contracting but maintaining adequate internal funding coverage.

Closing
Across all four areas, the institution stands as well-capitalized with improving profitability metrics, yet facing contractionary growth pressures on its balance sheet. Asset quality remains acceptable despite a modest increase in delinquency rates. Continued monitoring of asset quality will be important to ensure that loan performance does not deteriorate further amid ongoing member attrition and reduced lending activity.

loan.title: CEO Commentary on Loan Condition – As of May 31, 2026
loan.content:
Total loans stood at $19,912,409 as of May 31, 2026, reflecting an overall decline of -11.5% versus the prior year end. The portfolio is primarily concentrated in used vehicle loans and new vehicle loans, which together represent approximately 62.1% of total lending activity. Delinquency levels remain stable relative to historical trends.

Management continues to focus on credit quality through disciplined underwriting standards and ongoing monitoring protocols across all loan categories.

Portfolio Composition:
The largest segment by dollar size is used vehicle loans at $6,746,204 (33.9% of the portfolio), followed closely by new vehicle loans at $5,613,213 (28.2%). These two segments combined account for 62.1% of total loan balances.

Remaining smaller segments include all other unsecured loans/lines of credit ($5,049,676 or 25.4%), followed by secured non-real estate lines of credit secured by a first lien at $1,133,512 (5.7%). All other secured non-real estate loans/lines of credit comprise the smallest portion at $1,369,804 (6.9%).

Delinquency & Credit Quality:
Total delinquent loans amounted to $1,078,594 as of May 31, 2026.
The largest exposure within delinquencies is concentrated in used vehicle loans at $68,094 (representing approximately 6.3% of total delinquent balances), followed by new vehicle loans at $56,431 (~5.2%).

Delinquency concentration diverges from portfolio composition: while the majority of loan volume resides in used and new vehicle segments, these same categories also account for over half of all current delinquencies — specifically 68% when combined (used + new). This indicates that risk exposure is not evenly distributed across product lines.

Management maintains active oversight through regular review cycles and early intervention strategies to mitigate emerging credit issues before they escalate.

loan_continue.title: Charge-Offs & Recoveries
loan_continue.content:
The loss-and-recovery picture for this period reflects a significant contraction in charge-off activity across all segments.

Year-to-date gross charge-offs totaled $64,827. Losses were concentrated primarily within the All Other Unsecured Loans/Lines of Credit segment at $64,797, with smaller amounts recorded for New Vehicle Loans ($359) and Used Vehicle Loans ($30).

Recovery data was not disclosed in the source materials for this period.

Management will continue to focus on collections efforts and portfolio monitoring as charge-off activity remains elevated relative to prior periods.

share.title: CEO Commentary on Shares & Deposits: As of May 31, 2026
share.content:
Portfolio Composition
The credit union’s member shares totaled $48,291,158 as of May 31, 2026, reflecting a year-over-year decline of 4.0%. This contraction was driven by reductions across all major categories except for All Other Shares.
Regular Shares represented the largest funding component at $27,627,411 or 57.2% of total shares. Share Certificates followed as the second-largest category with a balance of $11,682,713, accounting for 24.2%. IRA/KEOGH Accounts comprised 14.8%, while Share Drafts and All Other Shares made up smaller portions at 3.4% and 0.4% respectively.
All major categories experienced declines: Regular Shares decreased by -3.5%, Share Certificates fell by -3.4%, IRA/KEOGH Accounts dropped significantly by -8.4%, and Share Drafts declined marginally by -1.0%. The only exception was All Other Shares, which grew substantially at 66.5%.
This period’s funding mix reflects a contraction in core share categories while the overall portfolio remains anchored by flexible Regular Shares. Management continues to focus on retaining member relationships and managing funding cost.

investment.title: Commentary on Investments: As of May 31, 2026
investment.content:
Portfolio Composition
As of May 31, 2026, total investments stood at $39,408,783, reflecting a 13.2% increase versus the prior year end. Held-to-Maturity Debt Securities comprised $39,299,007, representing approximately 99.7% of the portfolio, while Other Investments accounted for $109,776.

The growth in total investments was driven by a significant expansion in held-to-maturity debt securities over the reporting period. The source data does not provide information on cash balances or other asset categories to determine if this shift resulted from reallocating funds from those sources; therefore, only the observed increase is noted without attributing a specific cause.

Investment Maturity Distribution
The maturity structure of held-to-maturity debt securities was distributed across multiple horizons as shown in the chart. The portfolio included positions maturing within one year or less (blue), between 1 and 3 years (pink), between 3 and 5 years (green), between 5 and 10 years (purple), and greater than 10 years (light blue). This distribution suggests that some assets may support near-term liquidity needs, while others provide longer-dated income streams. However, because these are classified as held-to-maturity, their ability to be sold before maturity is not guaranteed by classification alone.

Overall Liquidity Role and Monitoring Focus
The portfolio’s composition—dominated by held-to-maturity debt securities—and its multi-horizon maturity structure may support the credit union’s liquidity position through a combination of predictable cash flows from maturing obligations and potential flexibility to sell select positions if needed. The institution will continue to monitor changes in market conditions that could affect either the availability or pricing of these assets, particularly given their classification as held-to-maturity.

membership.title:
Commentary on Membership
(Members data presented are as of 03-31-2026)
membership.content:
The dominant membership story for the period is one of stability in headcount, with a slight contraction relative to the prior quarter. As of March 31, 2026, Bopti Federal Credit Union reported 4,274 current members, representing a 0.2% increase year-over-year but an 0.8% decrease compared to the previous quarter.

The ratio of members per full-time employee has stabilized at approximately 950 following a significant decline in prior years and remains consistent with historical norms for this institution's size and service model, indicating that staffing levels are well-aligned with current member demand without signs of strain or excess capacity.

Shares-per-member have declined over the trailing twelve months. This downward trend indicates that existing members hold smaller average deposit balances than they did previously; consequently, even though total membership headcount has remained stable year-over-year, the funding base per individual member is contracting as account sizes shrink across the portfolio.

The period-specific assessment reflects a credit union with steady but slightly shrinking membership depth despite flat headcount. Continued focus on engagement and retention strategies will be essential to reverse the decline in shares-per-member and support growth in total deposits.

policy.title: Commentary on Policy/Limits Compliance: May 31, 2026
policy.content:
The credit union's compliance picture as of May 31, 2026 is largely compliant across most categories. However, the institution faces notable compliance gaps in several areas related to liquidity and funding mix, growth targets, net worth growth trajectory, and asset quality metrics.

Asset Quality
Delinquent loans represent a material concern at 1.73%, which exceeds the policy limit of less than 1.50%. However, classified assets relative to net worth remain within limits at -0.78% against a threshold of under 40%, and net charge-offs are contained at 0.50%, well below the benchmark of 0.75%.

Capital & Solvency
Net worth stands strong at 24.67% of total assets, significantly exceeding the minimum floor of 10%. The solvency evaluation is robust at 133.33%, comfortably above its target of 7%. However, net worth growth fell short of its year-over-year benchmark, recording 4.12% against a required threshold of at least 5%.

Profitability & Growth
The credit union's profitability metrics remain within established targets and limits; net margin is 2.95%, operating expenses are 1.91%, return on average assets reached 0.95%, cost of funds sits at 1.41%, yield on loans stands at 8.25%, and efficiency ratios hold steady at 2.11%. However, growth metrics show significant deviations from targets: loan growth contracted by -27.52% versus a target range of 5%-10%; market share declined by -11.03% against the benchmark to exceed market average; membership growth slipped into negative territory at -1.95%, missing its 3%-7% band.

Interest Rate & Funding
Liquidity and funding mix metrics are below their respective benchmarks, with cash and short-term investments comprising only 5.66% of total assets against a target range of 8%-15%; regular shares represent just 57.70% of the combined share and borrowing base versus a minimum floor of 70%. Total loans as a percentage of total assets sit at 31.28%, below its 65%-80% band, while total loans to total shares is recorded at 42.25%, missing its 70%-90% target range.

The credit union maintains strong capitalization and profitability but faces material headwinds in liquidity mix composition, growth trajectory across multiple dimensions, asset quality regarding delinquency levels, and net worth expansion pace. Management will prioritize addressing the elevated delinquent loan ratio to restore compliance with that specific limit while simultaneously executing a strategic plan to improve funding mix ratios and reverse negative growth trends.

ceo_summary.title: CEO Summary: Operational & Strategic Highlights (As of May 31, 2026)
ceo_summary.content:
**Portfolio Composition**
Bopti Federal Credit Union maintained adequate capital buffers this period with a net worth ratio of 24.67% and a solvency ratio of 1.33%, both exceeding regulatory thresholds for well-capitalized status, though the institution faces headwinds in growth trajectory targets. Profitability metrics remained within established parameters as return on average assets reached 0.95% and net interest margin improved to 2.85%. While these earnings supported a $265k increase in equity through retained capital, the primary structural challenge is that loan deployment contracted significantly while funding costs rose relative to growth targets.

The balance sheet experienced its largest absolute movement from loans and leases, which declined by approximately $4.7 million or 19.8% year-over-year, pulling total assets down slightly despite a broader 7.8% increase over the prior period. This contraction in lending activity was offset partially by investment securities rising $5.0 million to become the largest asset category at 61.7%, shifting the earning-asset mix away from traditional loans toward held-to-maturity debt holdings.

Asset quality indicators show a divergence between delinquency trends and charge-off performance, with the total delinquency ratio increasing to 1.73% while net charge-offs decreased to 0.50%. Credit stress is concentrated specifically in used vehicle loans at $68k of delinquent balances (representing approximately 6.3% of total delinquent amounts) and new vehicle loans at $56k (~5.2%), which together account for over half of all current delinquencies despite representing the majority of loan volume.

Funding dynamics reflect a contractionary environment where member shares declined by 4.0% year-over-year to reach $48,3 million, driven by reductions in regular shares (-3.5%) and IRA/KEOGH accounts (-8.4%). The cost of funds remained stable at the current level while total liabilities decreased primarily due to this drop in share balances, resulting in a core funding ratio that stayed near 57.7% despite missing broader growth targets.

Management priorities must focus on reversing negative loan and membership growth trends by addressing member attrition and shares-per-member declines identified earlier. Specific actions include reducing the elevated delinquency ratio of 1.73% to restore compliance with policy limits, particularly within used vehicle lending where risk exposure is highest. Additionally, leadership will execute a plan to rebalance liquidity mix composition toward target ranges for cash and short-term investments while improving net worth expansion pace against year-over-year benchmarks.

action_recommended.title: CEO's Actionable Recommendations (6-12 Months)
action_recommended.content:
**Credit quality and collections**
- Reduce delinquency exposure in used vehicle loans where $68,094 of balances represent approximately 6.3% of total delinquent amounts.
- Strengthen underwriting discipline for new vehicle loans which account for roughly 5.2% of current delinquencies alongside the larger segment.
- Monitor asset quality trends to ensure the rising total delinquency ratio of 1.73%, currently exceeding the policy limit, returns below the 1.50% threshold through enhanced collection protocols.

**Loan growth and asset utilization**
- Pursue targeted lending initiatives in used vehicle loans as this segment comprises $6,746,204 or 33.9% of total loan balances to reverse contractionary trends.
- Expand new vehicle loan origination efforts since these accounts for $5,613,213 (28.2%) and together with used vehicles drive the majority of portfolio volume.
- Optimize asset allocation by rebalancing away from held-to-maturity debt securities which now constitute 99.7% of investments to restore loans as a percentage of total assets toward the 65%-80% target range.

**Funding and membership**
- Strengthen member retention strategies for regular shares which declined by -3.5% year-over-year while representing $27,627,411 or 57.2% of total share balances.
- Review IRA/KEOGH account offerings as this category dropped significantly by -8.4%, contributing to the overall 4.0% decline in member shares.
- Expand engagement programs for All Other Shares which grew at 66.5% but remain a small portion, while addressing the broader contraction where total liabilities decreased primarily due to share balance drops.

**Profitability and earnings**
- Maintain net interest margin performance as it improved to 2.85%, ensuring continued contribution from Interest Income of $1,178,992 against Interest Expense of $393,101.
- Monitor non-interest income sources which totaled $61,283 and comprised primarily Fee Income ($44,516) to ensure they do not fall below 10% of total revenue again.
- Optimize operating expense management as Non-Interest Expense reached $560,366 with Employee Compensation at $44,516 requiring continued alignment with revenue scale.

**Capital and liquidity management**
- Maintain net worth ratio levels which stood strong at 24.67%, significantly exceeding the minimum floor of 10% while supporting capacity to absorb potential losses.
- Monitor solvency evaluation metrics as they remain robust at 133.33%, comfortably above the target of 7% despite headwinds in growth trajectory targets.
- Review liquidity mix composition where cash and short-term investments comprise only 5.66% of total assets against a target range of 8%-15% to address material gaps below benchmarks.
